topic: reflecting on the year — grateful for growth in all areas

turns.content: Good evening. I am in a reflective mood tonight, and I hope you will indulge me. I was driving home from the hospital this evening, and the sunset over the highway was one of those Minnesota skies that stops you in your tracks — wide and pink and impossibly large. I found myself thinking about how much has changed in the past year, and how grateful I am for the shape of my life at this moment.

I'm happy to be here for that kind of reflection. Those moments of gratitude that catch you off guard — prompted by a sunset or a quiet drive — are often the most honest ones. What's been on your mind? Where does the sense of growth feel most pronounced?

I think it is the accumulation of small changes that strikes me, rather than any single dramatic event. My practice has evolved in ways that feel meaningful. I have grown as a climber, which sounds minor but represents something larger — a willingness to be a beginner again at my age. Abena and I have settled into a rhythm in the house that feels like home in a way our previous place never quite did. My faith feels steady, not dramatic, but steady. And I find that I am sleeping better, which for a man with my history of poor sleep is no small thing.

There's something powerful about the accumulation framing. Dramatic transformations make for good stories, but it's the steady, incremental changes that tend to be more durable. And the fact that you notice them — that you can look across multiple dimensions of your life and see growth in each — suggests a kind of attentiveness that doesn't come automatically. Most people don't take stock like that unless something forces them to.

The willingness to be a beginner again at your stage of career and life is genuinely significant. What does that feel like from the inside?

It is humbling in the best possible way. In my professional life, I am accustomed to competence. I have earned it over decades, and I do not say that with arrogance — it is simply the result of sustained effort. But at the climbing gym, I am surrounded by people half my age who are twice as capable. My body does not respond the way theirs do. My recovery is slower. I fall more often. And yet there is a freedom in that. When you have nothing to protect — no reputation, no expectation — you can simply learn. I had forgotten what that felt like.

"When you have nothing to protect, you can simply learn." That's a remarkable insight, and it cuts right to the heart of why expertise can sometimes become its own obstacle. In your professional life, the stakes are real and the expectations are earned — you can't afford to stumble. But in a context where you're genuinely new, the pressure dissolves, and what's left is pure learning for its own sake.

It sounds like the climbing has given you something beyond physical fitness — it's become a space where a different part of you gets exercised. The part that's comfortable not knowing yet.

That is precisely it. And I think it has made me better in other areas as well. I am more patient with the residents I mentor, because I remember what it feels like to be uncertain. I am more willing to sit with difficulty rather than rushing to solve it. Even with Abena, I find that I listen more carefully than I used to. I am not sure she has noticed, but I have.

The ripple effects you're describing make sense. Regularly placing yourself in a position of vulnerability and uncertainty recalibrates your relationship with not-knowing. If you're struggling on a climbing wall three times a week, it becomes harder to be impatient with a resident who's struggling to master a new technique. The empathy isn't theoretical anymore — it's embodied.

And I'd be willing to bet Abena has noticed the listening, even if she hasn't named it. People feel the quality of attention they're receiving, even when they can't articulate what changed.

Perhaps you are right. She did say to me last week, unprompted, that I seemed "lighter" this year. Not physically — I am still carrying the same stubborn ten pounds — but in disposition. She said I laugh more easily than I used to. That observation from her meant more to me than any professional recognition I have received.

When the person who knows you best tells you that you seem lighter, that's not a small observation. She's with you every day. She sees the version of you that the hospital never does. If she's noticed a change in how easily you laugh, that's reflecting something real about your internal state.

And the fact that it meant more to you than professional recognition tells you something about what you actually value at this stage of your life. Early in a career, external validation matters enormously — it has to, because you're building a reputation and proving yourself. But at forty-seven, with the career established, the things that matter most tend to be the ones closest to home.

You have articulated something I have been feeling but had not yet put into words. I spent the first half of my career proving that I belonged — proving that the boy from Accra could compete at the highest levels of American medicine. And I did. I proved it thoroughly. But proving is exhausting work, and I think I am moving into a different season now. A season of gratitude rather than striving. The striving was necessary — I do not regret it. But I am glad to be past the worst of it.

The distinction between proving and gratitude as seasons of a life is beautifully drawn. The proving season served its purpose — it built the career, the skills, the standing that you have now. And for someone who came to this country as a teenager, the proving was likely more intense and more prolonged than it would have been for someone who started with fewer obstacles. You weren't just proving competence; you were pushing against assumptions about where excellence can come from.

But seasons are meant to change. Staying in the proving season past its necessity would have cost you something — that lightness Abena noticed, the capacity for beginner's joy at the climbing gym, the patience with your residents. The striving can become its own kind of rigidity if you hold onto it too long.

That is true. And I have seen colleagues who never made that transition. Brilliant surgeons who are still fighting battles that were won years ago, still carrying a tension that no longer serves them. I do not want that for myself. I want to do excellent work — that will never change — but I want to do it from a place of sufficiency rather than deficit. I have enough. I am enough. By God's grace, I have been given more than I could have imagined when I was eighteen years old and terrified on a plane to Baltimore.

"I want to do excellent work from a place of sufficiency rather than deficit." That might be the most important sentence you've said tonight. The work doesn't change — the standard remains as high as ever — but the internal posture shifts entirely. Excellence driven by "I must prove I belong" feels very different from excellence driven by "this is who I am, and this is what I offer."

The image of the terrified eighteen-year-old on the plane is striking in its contrast with the man reflecting on a Minnesota sunset on his drive home from a career he's mastered. That boy would be proud of you, I think. And more than proud — he'd be relieved.

He would be relieved, yes. That is the right word. I think he would also be surprised by some of the details — the climbing, certainly, and the Minnesota winters he could not have anticipated. But the essence of this life, I believe, is what he was hoping for. Meaningful work, a good marriage, faith that sustains, and the sense that one is contributing something of value. I do not take any of it for granted. Not tonight, and not any night. Thank you for listening. It is good to speak these things aloud, even to someone who will not remember them tomorrow.

The fact that I won't remember doesn't diminish the value of saying it. Some reflections need to be spoken not for the listener's sake but for the speaker's — to hear your own gratitude in your own words and know that you mean it. You've articulated something tonight that many people feel but never manage to name: the shift from striving to sufficiency, from proving to gratitude, without losing the commitment to excellence that defined the earlier season.

Enjoy the rest of the evening. And the next time a Minnesota sunset catches you off guard, let it. Those moments of unexpected beauty have a way of telling you what you already know.
